item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I’m glad you like my idea. Unfortunately, the article doesn’t currently meet rewrite criteria. I might ask the rewrite team if I can do it anyway at some point, but it’s old as dirt so I don’t even know who the original author is (I think far2 just moved it over from the old website).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I know a lot of time has gone by, but this short rewrite concept was still far more engaging and heartbreaking to me than the article itself. Would love to see it become a reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is poorly written.

  • The 16 by 16 meter room is pretty huge for someone that will break all their bones if they try to move.
  • Kevlar is an expensive material that I can't see a use beyond being written as a generic-strong-material-to-protect.
  • Having 8 staff members monitor this thing when it is known to cause mental decline would burn through personnel and would also mean a lot of staff would come in contact with this anomaly over the course of a year. Even one rogue agent could slip in and cause a security breach or theft (though goodness knows why they'd want to steal this particular anomaly).
  • Achilles procedure ∆ is listed in the containment procedures but is never elaborated upon. Presumably it's a way to neutralize this SCP
  • IQ is a really terrible way to measure intelligence. Historically it was the performance on a test and what was expected compared to their biological age. I don't know how much factors like stress, rest, and the knowledge of working around an anomaly that could lower your mental ability could swing the results but a bad performance might cause a false positive for the "anomalous" iq drain.
+ show block
– hide block
  • This person appears human but has bones way different than humans. I'm surprised the Foundation hasn't tried to sample this thing's DNA to see if it actually is human or not.

…the only language it speaks is a disrupted and broken version of English in which every word has been replaced with an almost entirely unconnected one. […] There seems to be no pattern to the word replacement, and attempts continue to translate it.

  • The linguistics of this are odd to me. If this person is producing random words with no connection to each other (e.g. "dephosphorization cat decrucifier" could have been a substitution of "I want food") then it's no different than just hitting the random entry button on a dictionary website, which is how I constructed that possible string.

It is obvious that what we say to it sounds just as garbled to it as what it says back to us does to us (if that makes any sense at all).

  • No it doesn't make sense. How is it obvious? What tests were done? It could be possible that this creature can receive input but is horribly bad at output.
  • Having guards physically alter themselves (filing of teeth and bone marrow transplants at the end) is again a waste of human resources when keeping this thing in a vegetative state, or just physically restrained would be more resource-effective and more likely to ensure secure containment.

-1


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, this is the creepiest SCP by far. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing the general sentiment that there's some potential with this one but as is it just amounts to mostly gibberish and cliched "mystery". My biggest issue though isn't the copious amounts of dead-end details like the thing with the teeth or the kevlar requirement. Instead, it's the stilted way it is written.

It feels very much like this was mixed together from numerous different sources, writing styles and ideas. Things like the random Greek character or the seemingly made-up protocol designations (perhaps these are a remnant of old SCP guidelines?). Rather than making the story told here feel more complete and "in-world" they shatter the fourth wall and the suspension of disbelief.

I hope that one of the other commenters that voiced an intention to get re-write approval for this actually does so because this could be a good skip I think. It has decent bones (forgive the pun) but as is it's "nearly as meaningless as the words he's supposed to be spouting" as UraniumEmpire put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
10/10 would rewrite if given the chance

A great idea executed terribly is still a great idea. This kid could make a great SCP if he had more of a personality, we actually heard some of the jumbled dialogue, his containment procedures weren't ridiculous, and all the other problems that people mentioned earlier weren't there.

Edit: And I know how I’d do it, too. Kind of like what 466 has, set up multiple tabs which are various versions of the file. First version is this one, but with the procedures toned down. Second version is explained (he just has brittle bone disease, living statue syndrome, and Wernicke’s Aphasia), so they decide to amnestize him and put the poor kid back wherever he came from. He finds out about that and tries to commit suicide, at which point his healing factor is discovered and he is reclassified as an anomaly. It’d be very tragic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is…quite bad. Walls of text, random imperial units, an anomaly nearly as meaningless as the words he's supposed to be spouting. I'm coming out of this perplexed that this survived this long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like there's an okay idea hiding behind this guy, but the way it is now is just riddled with nonsense. Why Kevlar? Why so many guards? Why file down canine teeth? This dude is easily broken how the hell do you think he could jump on someone and rip out teeth to kill himself when the very act of moving breaks his damn bones? Do you think the guards will bite him? Filing down canines won't do crap because I'm pretty sure he's so brittle blunt teeth will crush him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, this has aged very poorly. I fail to see why such extreme containment measures are needed, and the modification requiring things liked filed down canine teeth are absolutely ridiculous.
The anomalous nature stretches belief too. Brittle bone syndrome is a very real human condition. The same is true of Wernicke's aphasia, where people say nonsense words with the belief that they are making sense. The drop in IQ of people nearby can be explained by them having been led to expect this drop, and thus performing more poorly on IQ measures.

As it stands, this is a non-anomalous SCP with excessive containment procedures.
Edit: Except for the healing thing, but that's not the thing focused on in this article. The main anomaly is the speech thing, which is actually non-anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I get approval, I would like to try and do something with this.

Otherwise, it seems to merely survive from very old upvotes. The core object can be described with all the clinical precision needed in just a few lines, and everything else just does nothing really with it.

Well, almost nothing. The addenda makes this even more linked to SCP weaponization than most of the core SCPs and tales of the time. I'm not a fan of this, and am sorry that the only interesting line of the entire thing makes no sense as is in the Foundation.

Downvoted for good as it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why not change it to neutralized in light of the last excerpt suggesting self termination? Change it a bit?
Otherwise, downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I propose we allow SCP-116 to self-terminate in a controlled environment. This thing has, so far, proven to be a fruitless waste of resources.

This part is worth saving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, this thing's age is definitely showing, but I think there's an interesting object behind it all. This is an eldritch being: it is not meant to be and its very existence breaks down the sanity of those around it. Why or how exactly? We don't know, and that's part of its appeal.

Given how I currently have -three- four rewrites in the backburner (starvation bowl, guy that needs to make art to live, cube of destruction, and welcome mat), I really won't make any formal committal to fixing this, but I am sure if someone rewrote it, it'd be a hit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

SCP-116 is kept in a 16 by 16 meter cell, constructed of Kevlar

Why?

Cell is to be monitored at all times by 6 agents, 4 stationed at corner points of cell and 2 stationed with SCP-116

Why?

No pointed objects or intrusive testing equipment of any kind are to be placed in the possession of the agents within the cell

Uh

Outside monitoring is achieved using VBS05 class concealed button cameras stationed at high corners in the cell. Outside monitoring is to be carried out by a further 2 agents.

WHa

All monitoring agents, internal or external, are to undergo bi-monthly IQ tests as well as standard weekly psychiatric analysis. Significant drops in the IQ of agents (≥ 5 pts.) is to be regarded as prolonged exposure and to be treated as outlined in standard Quarantine instructions.

That's not even how IQ works, there are much more reliable ways to test for mental degradation. Additionally, why not use D class? Why not use a feeding tube, a drainage hole, and a spray nozzle and completely eliminate the need for personnel to interact with it at all?

SCP-116 has the outward appearance of a Caucasian male of around 9 years of age. Skin is cauterized and scarred over 98% of body, limbs, and head.

If 98% of their skin is cauterized and scarred, it'd be difficult to determine that they're Caucasian, no? This is what a near full-body burn victim looks like. Consider this your 'graphic content' warning.

http://i.dailymail.co.uk/i/pix/2014/01/03/article-2533154-1A656DCE00000578-425_634x422.jpg

I feel like that there's just nothing really worth salvaging out of this article. I read through it, looked it over again, and pretty much every other sentence I read would make me want to downvote it more. The containment procedures are just ridiculously over the top. It tries to do much and doesn't really have much of a hook or interesting core concept.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the bone/healing idea and I'm more or less neutral with the speaking. I'm not really getting why it affects others in the way it does though, the mental stuff feels somewhat tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures are hilariously over-the-top, and the effects of this person is really, really boring. Definite -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sad for the sake of sad. Downvote - let's help put the poor thing out of its misery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is the containment cell made of Kevlar, of all things? Are they afraid the SCP is gonna shoot the walls? Because if not, I don't see any other reason to have Kevlar in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it causes apparent permanent brain damage resulting in mental retardation in anyone lingering near it or trying to communicate with it. That tends to be utterly devastating to people, and to family and friends of the people affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand. Why do we even need to contain something like this? Because it can heal itself sort of fast? Because it talks weird? I'm not a fan, downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mostly I have no idea wtf the idea behind this one is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-116 has the outward appearance of a Caucasian male of around 9 years of age.

So he's actually a reptilian, then? Downboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll echo Drewbear. I'll add that there isn't really any reason for me to care about this object, as the only narrative is about how depressed it is.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
16x16 meter room. Composed of Kevlar. With 1m of rubber padding on the interior surfaces. Holy shit is that crappy containment.

Totally aside from that amazingly bad room, I find the guy's effects to be kinda meh. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. We have an object with unexplained properties that just wants to die and we have a Foundation which wants to be able to figure out the regeneration (which I suspect is directly tied to the fragile bones, something involving the binding components being leached away) but also recognizing that the cost may outweigh the benefits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: could have meant 'autopsy'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I propose we allow SCP-116 to self-terminate in a controlled environment. This thing has, so far, proven to be a fruitless waste of resources.

That's so cold. Love it. It would be even more abrupt if you removed "so far".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Authorised Suicide would be "Self-Termination".
But you're right on the unintentional bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP, but just one thing: There is no such thing as "unintentional termination", let alone self-termination. Termination is always purposeful and authorized.

item.title: I know this is an old entry... but...
item.content:encoded: This entry would probably benefit from an attached interview. If you need help with the writing of the dialogue for the SCP, you can look at something transcribed by a disorganized schizophrenic for inspiration or to use with permission. When undergoing an episode, their sentences make grammatical sense but are contextually nonsensical. As a result, reading it tricks the mind into thinking it should be able to understand it, and that the problem rests with the reader, not the writer. You can add a note at the beginning saying that the telepathic effect is lessened or disappears when transcribed and read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never noticed the bone marrow transplants before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Continued exposure to pain trains your pain receptors to respond more, not less. Your pain threshold goes down as you experience more pain, even if your pain tolerance increases.

Which makes this even worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Post-mortem biopsies are not possible. Biopsy means that the subject is alive for the procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about a sample of its wierd language?
